posts.title: Agents' Last Exam: Where Frontier AI Agents Score 2.6%
posts.subtitle: A 1,490-task benchmark built with 250+ industry experts finally tests agents on real jobs
posts.summary: Agents' Last Exam (ALE) is a new benchmark of 1,490 economically valuable tasks graded by verifiable outcomes instead of human judgment. On its hardest tier, even frontier agent stacks average a 2.6% full pass rate — a sharp reality check after benchmarks like SWE-bench started to saturate.

slides.title: The Benchmark Built to Break Agents
slides.body: UC Berkeley's RDI lab, working with 250+ industry experts, built Agents' Last Exam (ALE) because coding benchmarks like SWE-bench and Terminal-Bench were saturating while real professional work remained untested.

slides.title: Saturated Benchmarks Hid a Gap
slides.body: Agents already score around 82% on Terminal-Bench, but that measures narrow coding tasks — not the messy, multi-step professional work most jobs actually involve.
bullets: SWE-bench Verified rose from 1.96% (Oct 2023) to roughly 78% by spring 2026
High scores on coding benchmarks don't predict success on broader knowledge work
Most existing evals lean on subjective human grading, which doesn't scale or reproduce well

slides.title: Grounded in Real Occupations, Not Guesswork
slides.body: ALE's task taxonomy is built directly from the U.S. federal occupational taxonomy (O*NET/SOC 2018), clustering occupations with similar software-mediated workflows into 13 industry clusters and 55 subfields.
bullets: 1,490 task instances across 55 subdomains, built with 250+ industry experts
Every task has a verifiable, machine-checkable outcome — no subjective grading
Tasks interleave shell commands, GUI apps, file manipulation, and web research in one run
It's a 'living benchmark': the task pool keeps growing as new workflows are added

slides.title: What Running a Task Actually Involves
slides.body: ALE tasks mirror authentic professional workflows rather than toy problems — an agent has to chain together very different tool types and get graded purely on the final verifiable outcome.
bullets: A single task can require chaining shell commands, GUI actions, file edits, and web research end-to-end
Grading is fully automatic: outcomes are checked against a verifiable ground truth, not scored by a human reviewer
Cost and time signal the difficulty: one task run costs $3-10 in agent compute and can take tens of minutes to hours

slides.title: Why Verifiable Grading Matters
slides.body: Because outcomes are checked programmatically against ground truth rather than scored by a human or an LLM judge, ALE results are reproducible and harder to game — a real shift from benchmarks that lean on subjective grading.
bullets: No human-in-the-loop grading — outcomes are checked against a verifiable ground truth
Sidesteps the reproducibility problems that plague benchmarks graded by an LLM judge
Makes it possible to re-run and independently verify results across different agent harnesses

slides.title: Frontier Agents Hit a Wall
slides.body: Codex with GPT-5.5 hits about 82% on Terminal-Bench — but scores just 23.3% on ALE's Linux-only subset and 0% on the hardest Last-Exam tier. Claude Opus 4.8 and Gemini CLI both score a flat 0.0% on that same tier.

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: ALE tests real economically valuable work, not narrow coding puzzles — and grades it with verifiable outcomes, not human judgment
Frontier agents that ace Terminal-Bench and SWE-bench still average just 2.6% full-task success on ALE's hardest tier
Success doesn't transfer across benchmarks: 82% on Terminal-Bench became 0% on Last-Exam for the same agent stack
It's a living benchmark — expect the task pool and leaderboard to keep shifting as new industries are added

questions.prompt: What is unique about how ALE grades agent performance compared to many earlier agent benchmarks?
options: It uses a panel of AI judges
It grades outcomes against a verifiable ground truth rather than subjective human or LLM scoring
It only measures speed, not accuracy
It grades based on user surveys
questions.explanation: ALE tasks are designed so success can be checked programmatically against a ground-truth outcome, avoiding the inconsistency of subjective human or LLM-judge grading.

questions.prompt: On ALE's hardest 'Last-Exam' tier, what happened to Codex + GPT-5.5, which scores about 82% on Terminal-Bench?
options: It also scored about 82% on Last-Exam
It scored 0% on the Last-Exam tier
It refused to attempt any tasks
It scored higher than on Terminal-Bench
questions.explanation: Despite dominating Terminal-Bench, the same configuration scored 0% on ALE's hardest tier — strong performance on narrow coding benchmarks doesn't transfer to broad, real-world professional tasks.

questions.prompt: How is ALE's task taxonomy structured?
options: Randomly sampled from internet forums
Grounded in the U.S. federal O*NET/SOC 2018 occupational taxonomy, covering 13 industry clusters
Based only on software engineering tasks
Limited to a single industry
questions.explanation: ALE's 1,490 tasks are organized into 13 industry clusters and 55 subfields derived from the O*NET/SOC 2018 occupational taxonomy, covering real professional workflows across many industries.

references.title: Agents' Last Exam (arXiv:2606.05405)

posts.title: MolmoMotion: Ai2 Teaches AI to Predict Motion Before It Happens
posts.subtitle: Give it a point, an object, and a sentence — it forecasts where that point goes in 3D
posts.summary: Ai2 released MolmoMotion, an open model that predicts how 3D points on an object will move over the next few seconds given a video frame and a language instruction — without rendering any future video. It ships with an open dataset of over a million trajectories and a new benchmark, aimed at robotics planning and trajectory-conditioned video generation.

slides.title: Motion Forecasting Without Rendering Video
slides.body: Ai2's MolmoMotion predicts how marked 3D points on an object will move over the next few seconds — given only a video frame, the points, and a plain-language instruction like 'move and rotate the wooden bowl with fruit on the table.'

slides.title: Predicting Motion Usually Means Generating Video
slides.body: Most systems forecast future motion by generating full future video frames pixel by pixel — expensive and overkill when all you actually need is where an object's points will end up.
bullets: Rendering full video is computationally expensive just to find out where an object goes
Pixel-space predictions don't map directly onto the 3D coordinates robots or physics engines need
Motion models tied to human-body or rigid-object templates don't generalize to arbitrary objects

slides.title: Represent Motion as 3D Points, Not Pixels
slides.body: MolmoMotion represents motion as object-attached 3D points in world space — designed to be class-agnostic (works on any object, not just hands or rigid bodies), view-stable (consistent across camera angles), and directly usable by downstream robotics or video systems.
bullets: Input: an RGB frame, 3D query points on the object, and a language instruction
Output: each point's predicted 3D trajectory in a metric world frame over the next few seconds
Built on the Molmo 2 vision-language backbone

slides.title: Two Ways to Predict a Trajectory
slides.body: Ai2 shipped two variants: MolmoMotion-AR, which decodes trajectories as quantized coordinate text token by token, and MolmoMotion-FM, which uses flow matching to predict continuous 3D coordinates directly — better suited to instructions with multiple plausible futures.
bullets: MolmoMotion-AR: autoregressive, quantized coordinates as text
MolmoMotion-FM: flow-matching, continuous coordinates, models uncertainty
Both consume the same image, text, and query-point tokens from the Molmo 2 encoder

slides.title: From Instruction to Trajectory
slides.body: Mark a point on a bowl and give the instruction 'move and rotate the wooden bowl with fruit on the table.' MolmoMotion outputs a predicted 3D path for that point over the next few seconds — a trajectory a robot arm's planner or a video generator can condition on directly, with no rendered frames in between.

slides.title: Beating the Alternatives on PointMotionBench
slides.body: Ai2 built PointMotionBench, a human-verified benchmark spanning 111 object categories and 61 motion types, specifically to test this. MolmoMotion beat pixel-space video generators, parametric-3D motion models, and simple constant-velocity extrapolation on it.

rows: Renders future frames, then infers motion
Assumes points keep moving in a straight line

slides.title: Know the Limits
slides.body: MolmoMotion is a forecasting model, not a full world simulator — it's most useful paired with a system that can act on its predictions.
bullets: It predicts point trajectories, not appearance — you still need a separate renderer or robot controller to act on the prediction
Ambiguous instructions with multiple valid outcomes are exactly where MolmoMotion-FM's uncertainty modeling matters most; MolmoMotion-AR just picks one path
Class-agnostic doesn't mean flawless — that's why PointMotionBench exists, since failure modes still vary by object and motion type

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: MolmoMotion predicts 3D point trajectories directly from an image and a language instruction, skipping full video rendering
It ships fully open: model weights, the MolmoMotion-1M dataset (built from 1.16M videos), and the PointMotionBench benchmark
Built on Molmo 2, with two variants trading off simplicity (autoregressive) against uncertainty modeling (flow matching)
Aimed squarely at robotics planning and trajectory-conditioned video generation

questions.prompt: What does MolmoMotion actually predict, given an image, 3D points, and a language instruction?
options: A full rendered video of the future
The future 3D trajectory of the marked points in a metric world frame
A text description of the motion
A 2D bounding box around the object
questions.explanation: MolmoMotion skips full video generation and directly forecasts where each marked 3D point will move over the next few seconds, in real-world (metric) coordinates.

questions.prompt: Why does Ai2 describe its point representation as 'class-agnostic'?
options: It only works on rigid objects
It doesn't rely on templates specific to hands, bodies, or any fixed object category
It requires labeled object classes at inference time
It only works for human motion
questions.explanation: Unlike motion models built around specific templates (e.g., hand or human-body models), MolmoMotion's object-attached 3D points work for any object without category-specific templates.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between the MolmoMotion-AR and MolmoMotion-FM variants?
options: AR is for video, FM is for images
AR decodes trajectories as quantized text tokens; FM uses flow matching over continuous coordinates and better models uncertainty
They are the same model with different names
FM is only for 2D motion
questions.explanation: MolmoMotion-AR treats trajectory prediction like autoregressive text generation over quantized coordinates, while MolmoMotion-FM predicts continuous coordinates via flow matching, which better captures cases with multiple plausible futures.

references.title: MolmoMotion: Language-guided 3D motion forecasting (Ai2 blog)
MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction (arXiv:2606.18558)

posts.title: Reranking: The Second Pass That Fixes RAG's Blind Spots
posts.subtitle: Your vector search found 50 candidates — reranking figures out which ones actually answer the question
posts.summary: Bi-encoder vector search is fast but blind to how a query and document actually interact. Reranking adds a second, slower pass with a cross-encoder that scores each query-document pair jointly, reordering the top candidates for much better precision — at the cost of extra latency.

slides.title: Vector Search Gets You Close, Not Right
slides.body: Retrieval-augmented generation pipelines often nail topical similarity but miss precise relevance — that's the gap a reranking stage is built to close.

slides.title: Bi-Encoders Are Fast but Blind
slides.body: Standard vector search encodes the query and every document independently into embeddings, then ranks by cosine similarity. That's what makes it fast enough for millions of documents — and also its core weakness.
bullets: Query and document embeddings are computed separately, with no interaction between them
Two passages can look 'similar' by embedding distance while differing in what actually answers the query
Errors surface as topically-relevant-but-wrong-answer chunks ranked above the correct one

slides.title: Cross-Encoders Score the Pair Together
slides.body: A cross-encoder reranker feeds the query and a candidate document into the model together, in a single forward pass, so attention can compare them token by token — producing a far more accurate relevance score than comparing two independent embeddings.
bullets: Bi-encoder: encode query and doc separately, then compare via cosine similarity (fast, ANN-indexable)
Cross-encoder: encode query and doc jointly, output one relevance score (slow, can't be pre-indexed)
The joint pass is what lets it catch relevance signals a separate-embedding comparison misses

slides.title: The Two-Stage Pipeline
slides.body: Because cross-encoders are too slow to run over an entire corpus, production RAG systems use them only on a narrowed shortlist.
bullets: Stage 1 (bi-encoder): retrieve the top 50-100 candidates from the full corpus via ANN search
Stage 2 (cross-encoder): rerank just those candidates, keep the top 5-10 for the LLM's context
This keeps overall latency low while fixing precision exactly where it's needed

slides.title: Same Query, Different Order
slides.body: A user asks a support bot: 'Can I cancel my subscription after the trial ends?' Bi-encoder search might rank a general 'About our subscription plans' passage above the specific 'Cancellation policy' passage, since both discuss subscriptions similarly. A cross-encoder reranker, scoring the query against each passage jointly, recognizes that only the cancellation-policy passage actually answers the question — and moves it to #1.

slides.title: What Reranking Costs vs. Buys You
slides.body: Reranking doesn't replace vector search — it corrects its ordering on a shortlist small enough to afford the extra compute.

rows: Broad recall across millions of docs
Tens to hundreds of ms per shortlist
Precision on a small candidate set

slides.title: When Reranking Isn't Worth It
slides.body: Reranking is a precision tool, not a free lunch — it adds a model call and latency to every query.
bullets: Ultra-low-latency use cases (sub-100ms budgets) may not be able to afford a second model pass
If your corpus is small enough to rerank everything directly, you may not need a separate fast retrieval stage at all
A cross-encoder can't fix retrieval that missed the right document entirely — it only reorders what stage 1 already surfaced
It's an extra model to host, monitor, and pay for in your pipeline

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Bi-encoders retrieve fast by comparing independent embeddings; cross-encoders rerank accurately by scoring query and document jointly
The standard pattern: retrieve top 50-100 with a bi-encoder, rerank down to top 5-10 with a cross-encoder
Reranking fixes ordering, not recall — if the right document never makes the shortlist, reranking can't rescue it
Skip it when latency budgets are extremely tight or your corpus is small enough to score directly

questions.prompt: Why is a bi-encoder used for the first retrieval stage instead of a cross-encoder?
options: Bi-encoders are more accurate
Bi-encoder document embeddings can be precomputed and searched via ANN, making them fast enough for large corpora
Cross-encoders can't process text
Bi-encoders require no training
questions.explanation: Because a bi-encoder embeds the query and documents independently, document embeddings can be precomputed and indexed for fast approximate nearest-neighbor search — something a cross-encoder can't do, since it needs the query at scoring time.

questions.prompt: What does a cross-encoder reranker do differently from a bi-encoder?
options: It skips the query entirely
It encodes the query and each candidate document together in one forward pass, letting them attend to each other
It only works on images
It replaces the need for any retrieval step
questions.explanation: A cross-encoder processes the query-document pair jointly, so the model can directly compare tokens between them — producing a more precise relevance score than comparing two separately-computed embeddings.

questions.prompt: If the correct document is never retrieved in stage 1, what can reranking do about it?
options: It will still surface the correct document from the rest of the corpus
Nothing — reranking can only reorder documents that stage 1 already retrieved
It automatically expands the search to the full corpus
It replaces stage 1 with a full corpus scan
questions.explanation: Reranking only reorders the shortlist that the first retrieval stage already produced — it can't recover documents that were never retrieved in the first place, which is why recall at stage 1 still matters.

references.title: How Using a Reranking Microservice Can Improve Accuracy and Costs of Information Retrieval (NVIDIA)

posts.title: OPID: Teaching AI Agents From Their Own Past Mistakes
posts.subtitle: A hindsight-skill trick that turns finished agent runs into denser training signal
posts.summary: OPID (On-Policy Skill Distillation) is a June 2026 training technique that mines completed agent trajectories for hierarchical hindsight skills — episode-level workflow rules and step-level decision fixes — then folds them back in as dense, token-level training signal. It improves over outcome-only RL and prior skill-distillation baselines on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-based QA, without adding any retrieval or analysis step at inference time.

slides.title: When Agents Only Learn From Win or Lose
slides.body: An LLM agent might take 20+ steps — plan, tool call, observe, replan — before a task resolves. Standard RL only tells it whether the ending was good. OPID asks: what if, after the fact, we extracted lessons from every step in between?

slides.title: Outcome Rewards Are Too Sparse to Teach Anything
slides.body: Outcome-only RL (plain GRPO/PPO-style training over a full rollout) assigns one reward to an entire trajectory and spreads it across every token equally.
bullets: A successful ALFWorld/WebShop/Search-QA trajectory can span dozens of tool calls — some useful, many wasted
A single end-of-trajectory reward can't tell a sharp decision from a wandering one; both get the same credit
Prior skill-distillation fixes exist, but need a retrieval step or privileged context AT INFERENCE time — added latency baked into every deployed call

slides.title: Hindsight Skills, Extracted After the Trajectory Ends
slides.body: Once a rollout finishes, an LLM analyzer looks back over the whole trajectory and distills two kinds of skills from it — no privileged information, just hindsight.
bullets: Episode-level skills: global workflow or failure-avoidance rules ('check the room map before searching')
Step-level skills: local decision knowledge tied to one specific, critical timestep
Critical-first routing: step-level skills fire only at identified critical decisions; everywhere else, the episode-level skill is the default guidance

slides.title: Turning Hindsight Into a Token-Level Signal
slides.body: The extracted skill text is injected into the interaction history, and the OLD policy re-scores the exact same sampled response under both the original and the skill-augmented context.
bullets: The resulting log-probability shift becomes a token-level self-distillation advantage
That advantage is combined with the standard outcome advantage during policy optimization
Because skills only ever touch the training-time re-scoring step, the deployed policy needs no analyzer, retrieval, or extra context at inference

rows: One reward for the whole trajectory
None — skills only used during training

slides.title: Worked Example: The Wandering Apple
slides.body: Task: 'put a hot apple on the counter.' The agent picks up the apple correctly, then spends 6 extra steps searching the wrong room for a microwave before finally succeeding.
bullets: Outcome-only RL sees a success and reinforces the ENTIRE trajectory equally — including the 6 wasted steps
OPID's analyzer flags the wrong-room search as a step-level lesson ('check the room map before searching') at that critical timestep
It still credits the correct apple-heating sequence at the episode level, so the fix is targeted, not blanket

slides.title: Where OPID Costs You
slides.body: The gains come with real constraints, not a free lunch.
bullets: Needs an LLM analyzer to generate hindsight skills during training, adding compute/API cost to the training loop itself
Validated on three tool-use environments — ALFWorld, WebShop, Search-based QA — not yet shown on open-ended agentic coding or long-horizon tasks
It's a training-time fix: a bad base policy or a badly shaped environment reward isn't solved by better hindsight alone

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Hindsight skill extraction turns one sparse outcome reward into dense, token-level supervision
Critical-first routing keeps the analyzer's attention on the decisions that actually mattered
All the extra machinery lives in training — the deployed policy runs exactly as before, with no added latency
Code (MIT-licensed) and an ALFWorld checkpoint are public, released alongside the paper on 2026-06-25

questions.prompt: What signal does outcome-only RL give an agent across a multi-step trajectory?
options: A separate reward at every single token
One reward for the whole trajectory, applied equally to every step
Human feedback on each tool call
No reward — only imitation learning
questions.explanation: Outcome-only RL gives one success/fail signal per rollout, spread evenly across every step — which is exactly what OPID's hindsight skills are designed to sharpen.

questions.prompt: What is a 'step-level' skill in OPID?
options: A global rule about the whole episode's workflow
Local decision knowledge extracted at one specific, critical timestep
A retrieval index queried at inference time
A learning-rate schedule
questions.explanation: Step-level skills capture what should have happened at one critical decision point; critical-first routing applies them only there, falling back to episode-level skills elsewhere.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't OPID slow down inference, unlike some earlier skill-distillation methods?
options: It quantizes the model
Skills are only used during training to reshape the learning signal, never injected at inference
It uses a smaller model at inference
It removes the reward model entirely
questions.explanation: Earlier skill-distillation approaches need an analyzer or retrieval step at inference time; OPID folds skill use entirely into training via a token-level self-distillation advantage, so the deployed policy is unchanged.

references.title: OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning (arXiv:2606.26790)
jinyangwu/OPID — official code and ALFWorld checkpoint

posts.title: FlashAttention: Rewriting Attention for the Memory Wall
posts.subtitle: Same math, computed so the full attention matrix never has to touch slow memory
posts.summary: FlashAttention computes the exact same attention output as standard implementations, but restructures the computation around the GPU memory hierarchy — tiling Q/K/V into blocks and using a running softmax so the full N x N score matrix never gets written to slow HBM. That IO-awareness, not a new approximation, is what let context windows grow from a few thousand tokens to well past 100K without a proportional memory blowup.

slides.title: The Bottleneck Was Never the Math
slides.body: Standard self-attention is exact — the slowdown doesn't come from the floating-point operations. It comes from writing a giant intermediate matrix to memory and reading it straight back.

slides.title: Attention's Real Cost Is Memory Traffic, Not FLOPs
slides.body: Self-attention computes an N x N score matrix (QK^T), applies softmax, then multiplies by V.
bullets: For N = 8,000 tokens, that's a 64-million-entry matrix — per head, per layer
GPU compute (FLOPs) has scaled far faster than GPU memory bandwidth (HBM), so attention is memory-bound, not compute-bound
A naive implementation writes the full N x N matrix to slow HBM, then reads it back twice more — once for softmax, once for the final matmul

slides.title: Tiling + Online Softmax: Never Materialize the Full Matrix
slides.body: FlashAttention splits Q, K, and V into small blocks that fit in the GPU's fast on-chip SRAM, and computes attention block-by-block.
bullets: A running ('online') softmax rescales partial sums as each new block arrives, so no block ever needs the full row of scores at once
The full N x N matrix is never written to HBM — only O(N) memory is needed instead of O(N^2)
This is an IO-aware algorithm redesign, not a lower-precision or sparse approximation — the output is bit-for-bit the same attention computation

slides.title: One Fused Kernel Instead of Three Separate Passes
slides.body: QK^T, the softmax rescaling, and the PV matmul all happen inside a single CUDA kernel per tile, keeping intermediate results resident in SRAM the whole time.
bullets: Fusing the three steps avoids the extra HBM round-trips a naive, op-by-op implementation pays for
The backward pass recomputes attention on the fly instead of storing it — classic gradient-checkpointing, applied specifically to the attention matrix
Recomputing is cheap on a GPU with FLOPs to spare; storing an O(N^2) matrix in HBM and reading it back is the expensive part

rows: O(N^2) — full matrix written to HBM
O(N) — running softmax, no full matrix stored
Multiple passes over an O(N^2) matrix
One fused pass per tile, mostly in SRAM
Same exact attention math — no approximation

slides.title: Worked Example: A 32K-Token Context
slides.body: N = 32,768 tokens means N^2 ≈ 1.07 billion score matrix entries.
bullets: At fp16 (2 bytes each), that's ~2.1GB for ONE attention matrix — per head, per layer — far too big to keep in SRAM and expensive to shuttle through HBM repeatedly
Tiling into, say, 128-token blocks means only a 128 x 128 tile (16,384 entries, 32KB) is resident at any moment
That tile comfortably fits in the GPU's on-chip SRAM, which is exactly why the full matrix never needs to leave fast memory

slides.title: When It Doesn't Help
slides.body: FlashAttention is not a universal speedup button.
bullets: It doesn't reduce FLOPs — on short sequences (a few hundred tokens) where compute already dominates, the memory-traffic savings barely register
It needs custom, low-level kernels tuned per GPU architecture, so support can lag on new or non-NVIDIA hardware
It's exact-attention only — it won't help if what you actually need is a sparse or linear-attention approximation for better asymptotic scaling
Gains shrink if the model is bottlenecked elsewhere — small batch sizes, CPU-bound data loading, or a memory-bound decode step

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: FlashAttention is exact, not approximate — identical output to standard attention, just computed IO-aware
Tiling + online softmax + recomputation together target the GPU memory hierarchy, not the underlying math
It's a big reason context windows could grow from ~2-4K tokens to 100K+ without a proportional memory blowup
Later versions (FlashAttention-2, -3) push the same idea further: better GPU occupancy and newer hardware support (H100 tensor cores, FP8)

questions.prompt: What is the main bottleneck FlashAttention targets?
options: The number of floating-point operations in attention
Memory traffic between GPU HBM and on-chip SRAM
The size of the model's vocabulary
Network latency between GPUs
questions.explanation: Attention is memory-bound on modern GPUs — FLOPs have scaled faster than memory bandwidth, so cutting HBM read/writes matters more than cutting compute.

questions.prompt: How does FlashAttention avoid ever storing the full N x N attention matrix?
options: It approximates attention with a sparse pattern
It processes Q/K/V in small tiles and maintains a running 'online' softmax
It reduces the sequence length before computing attention
It uses fewer attention heads
questions.explanation: Tiling plus an online (running) softmax lets FlashAttention compute exact attention block-by-block in fast SRAM, rescaling partial results as each new tile arrives.

questions.prompt: Why does FlashAttention recompute attention scores during the backward pass instead of storing them?
options: Recomputing is mathematically required for correctness
It trades cheap extra compute for avoiding an expensive O(N^2) memory store
Storage isn't supported during training
It only applies to inference, not training
questions.explanation: GPUs are compute-rich but memory-bandwidth-constrained, so recomputing the attention matrix on the fly during backprop beats paying the HBM cost of storing and re-reading it — classic gradient checkpointing applied to attention.

references.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness (arXiv:2205.14135)

posts.title: SelfCompact: Let the Agent Decide When to Forget
posts.subtitle: A rubric-driven compaction tool beats fixed-interval summarization at a fraction of the tokens
posts.summary: SelfCompact is a June 2026 scaffold that lets an LLM agent decide, mid-trajectory, when its own context should be compacted rather than relying on a fixed token-count trigger. By pairing a compaction tool with a rubric for when to fire versus suppress, it matches or beats fixed-interval summarization while cutting token usage 30-70% and lifting accuracy up to 18.1 points on math tasks.

slides.title: An Agent's Context Window Is a Leaky Bucket
slides.body: Long agent trajectories — chains of thought interleaved with tool calls — pile up stale content fast. A June 2026 paper asks: instead of a timer deciding when to clean up, why not let the model decide?

slides.title: Fixed-Interval Compaction Cuts at the Wrong Moments
slides.body: Most agent scaffolds compact context whenever token count crosses a threshold — say, every 20k tokens. That trigger knows nothing about what the agent is actually doing.
bullets: A threshold can fire mid-derivation, discarding a partial proof or half-finished search before it resolves
It can also fire mid-tool-call-chain, losing the exact state needed to interpret the next tool result
Stale content that never gets compacted keeps 'anchoring' generation — the model keeps attending to context that no longer matters
Eventually the trajectory just outgrows the context window and the run fails outright

slides.title: SelfCompact: A Tool Plus a Rubric
slides.body: SelfCompact gives the model two things instead of a timer: a tool it can call to summarize its own accumulated context, and a lightweight rubric for when that's a good idea.
bullets: Compaction tool: the model itself invokes it to compress the trajectory so far into a summary
Fire condition: a sub-task has just resolved, or the trajectory is visibly converging on an answer
Suppress condition: the model is mid-derivation or currently stuck — compacting here would erase exactly the state it needs
Net effect: compaction becomes a decision tied to task structure, not to a token counter

slides.title: Why Structure-Awareness Matters
slides.body: The key insight isn't the summarization step itself — every scaffold can summarize. It's giving the model the judgment call about timing, which only the agent tracking its own reasoning actually has access to.
bullets: A fixed-interval trigger is blind to task boundaries; SelfCompact's rubric is built around them
Because the model chooses when to fire, most trajectories need far fewer compaction calls overall
Fewer, better-timed compactions mean less redundant re-summarization of content that was about to become irrelevant anyway

slides.title: The Numbers, Against a No-Summarization Baseline
Worked Example: A Multi-Step Math Proof
slides.body: Picture an agent working a multi-lemma proof. A fixed-interval scaffold hits its 20k-token threshold right after lemma 2 is half-derived — and compacts, losing the intermediate algebra.
bullets: SelfCompact's rubric recognizes 'lemma 2 not yet resolved' and suppresses compaction there
It fires instead right after lemma 2 closes cleanly, folding it into a short summary before starting lemma 3
The agent never re-derives lost work, and the context stays small enough to reach lemma 4 and 5 without truncation

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Fixed-interval context compaction is structurally blind — it can't tell mid-derivation from a clean stopping point
Giving the model a compact tool plus a fire/suppress rubric turns compaction into a task-aware decision
Result: up to 18.1 points higher accuracy on math and 5-9 on agentic search, at 30-70% lower token cost than no summarization
This is a scaffold-level fix, not a model-level one — it's addable to any agent loop that already supports tool calls

questions.prompt: What's the core problem with fixed-interval context compaction in long agent trajectories?
options: It uses too much GPU memory
It triggers based on token count, not task structure, so it can fire mid-derivation and discard needed state
It only works for coding agents
It requires a second, larger model to run
questions.explanation: A token-count trigger has no idea whether the agent is mid-proof or between sub-tasks — it can wipe out exactly the state the next step depends on.

questions.prompt: What two components make up SelfCompact?
options: A bigger context window and a faster tokenizer
A separate summarization model and a retry loop
A compaction tool the model can invoke, plus a rubric for when to fire vs. suppress it
A vector database that stores old context externally
questions.explanation: SelfCompact pairs an inference-time tool call with a rubric tied to task state (resolved sub-task or convergence = fire; mid-derivation or stuck = suppress).

questions.prompt: Roughly how does SelfCompact's token cost compare to fixed-interval summarization, while matching or beating its accuracy?
options: About the same token cost
2-3x higher token cost
30-70% lower token cost
It eliminates token cost entirely by skipping summarization
questions.explanation: Because the model only compacts at well-timed moments instead of on every threshold crossing, it needs far fewer (and more useful) summarization calls.

posts.title: Claude Fable 5: Anthropic's Mythos-Class Model Goes Public
posts.subtitle: The safeguarded flagship tier finally ships to everyone, with SWE-Bench Pro at 80.3%
posts.summary: On July 1, 2026, Anthropic made Claude Fable 5 generally available — the first public release from its more capable 'Mythos-class' tier, alongside a restricted sibling, Claude Mythos 5, for vetted cyber-defense use. Fable 5 posts state-of-the-art results across coding, knowledge work, and document reasoning, while routing a small fraction of sensitive queries to Opus 4.8 as a safety measure.

slides.title: A New Tier Above Opus, Finally Public
slides.body: Anthropic has run a more powerful 'Mythos-class' model internally and under export controls since June. On July 1, the safeguarded public version — Claude Fable 5 — opened up to everyone.

slides.title: Why It Wasn't Available Sooner
slides.body: Mythos-class models were held back by a US export-control order issued in mid-June, which pulled the model family offline for roughly three weeks before restrictions were lifted on June 30.
bullets: Claude Mythos 5 — the same underlying model with more safeguards lifted — stays restricted to a small group of vetted cyber-defenders and infrastructure providers
Claude Fable 5 is the generally-available sibling: same capability tier, extra guardrails for the public API and Claude.ai
This two-tier release pattern (broad model + restricted sibling) is new territory for how frontier labs ship their most capable systems

slides.title: What Actually Shipped
slides.body: Fable 5 is available across the Claude Platform, Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, positioned as Anthropic's most capable generally-available model to date.
bullets: Software engineering, knowledge work, vision, and scientific-research tasks all see meaningful jumps over Opus 4.8
Pro, Max, Team, and select Enterprise plans got Fable 5 at up to 50% of weekly usage limits through July 7, then usage-credit pricing after
Safety routing: queries on certain sensitive topics are silently redirected to Opus 4.8 instead of Fable 5, triggering in under 5% of sessions

slides.title: Where the Gain Shows Up Day-to-Day
slides.body: Anthropic's own comparison highlights everyday spreadsheet work, not just headline coding benchmarks.
bullets: Fable 5 beats Opus 4.8 on spreadsheet tasks at every effort level tested
It does so in fewer turns — finishing runs 25-30% faster than Opus 4.8 on the same tasks
Combined with the SWE-Bench Pro jump, the pitch is less 'smarter' and more 'finishes real multi-step work with fewer retries'

slides.title: Trade-offs and Open Questions
slides.body: A safeguarded public tier plus a restricted sibling model is a genuinely new distribution pattern, and it comes with caveats worth tracking.
bullets: Export-control exposure: the model was pulled offline once already; availability isn't guaranteed to be stable long-term
Safety routing means the model you get is sometimes silently swapped to Opus 4.8 — behavior can vary run to run on sensitive prompts
Promotional usage limits (50% of weekly cap through July 7) are temporary; budget for standard usage-credit pricing after
Benchmark numbers above come from Anthropic's own release plus independent outlets — treat exact percentages as vendor-reported until third-party evals catch up

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Claude Fable 5 is Anthropic's first generally-available model from its Mythos-class tier, live since July 1, 2026
It leads on SWE-Bench Pro (80.3% vs. GPT-5.5's 58.6%) and several knowledge-work and document-reasoning benchmarks
A more capable sibling, Mythos 5, stays restricted to vetted cyber-defense use — capability and access are now being split, not just capability tiers
Try it via Claude Code, Claude.ai, or the Claude Platform API; expect occasional silent routing to Opus 4.8 on sensitive queries

options: Mythos 5 is an older, smaller model
They're unrelated models from different training runs
Same underlying Mythos-class model, but Mythos 5 has more safeguards lifted and stays restricted to vetted cyber-defense use
Fable 5 is text-only while Mythos 5 adds vision
questions.explanation: Fable 5 and Mythos 5 share the same capability tier; Mythos 5 is the less-restricted variant limited to a small vetted group rather than the general public.

Roughly 22 points higher (80.3% vs 58.6%)
They scored identically
GPT-5.5 scored higher
questions.explanation: Fable 5 reached 80.3% versus GPT-5.5's 58.6% on SWE-Bench Pro — a large gap on this software-engineering benchmark, per Anthropic's release comparison.

questions.prompt: What safety mechanism does Anthropic use with Fable 5's public deployment?
options: It refuses all sensitive queries outright
It silently routes some sensitive-topic queries to Claude Opus 4.8 instead, in under 5% of sessions
It requires manual human approval for every response
It disables tool use for all users
questions.explanation: Rather than a hard refusal, Fable 5 routes a small fraction of queries on sensitive topics to Opus 4.8 — a behind-the-scenes swap that triggers in less than 5% of sessions.

posts.title: Rotary Positional Embeddings: How LLMs Know Word Order
posts.subtitle: RoPE encodes position as a rotation, not an addition — and that's why it extrapolates
posts.summary: Attention has no built-in sense of word order — every token looks the same regardless of position unless you tell it otherwise. Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) inject position by rotating query and key vectors, so relative distance falls naturally out of the dot product. It's why most modern open LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen) extrapolate to longer contexts far better than older absolute-position schemes.

slides.title: Attention Is Order-Blind by Default
slides.body: Self-attention computes a weighted sum over all tokens using dot products. Shuffle the input tokens and, without positional information, you'd get the exact same output — a transformer literally cannot tell 'dog bites man' from 'man bites dog'.

slides.title: Early Fixes Didn't Generalize
slides.body: The original Transformer added fixed sinusoidal position vectors to token embeddings. Later models learned absolute position embeddings from scratch instead.
bullets: Absolute embeddings tie a token's representation to its exact index — position 500 has a totally different vector than position 5,000
A model trained on 2k-token sequences has never seen a 'position 5,000' embedding at all, so it can't extrapolate past training length
What actually matters for meaning is usually relative distance between tokens ('3 words apart'), not their absolute index — absolute schemes don't encode that directly

slides.title: RoPE's Idea: Encode Position as a Rotation
slides.body: RoPE (Su et al., 2021) doesn't add a position vector to the embedding. It rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their position, in pairs of dimensions, before the dot product is taken.
bullets: Each pair of dimensions in Q and K gets rotated by θ·position, where θ varies by dimension pair (like the frequencies in sinusoidal encoding)
The rotation is applied at attention time, not baked into the token embedding itself
Crucially, rotating both Q and K means their dot product depends only on the relative rotation — i.e., the distance between the two positions — not on either absolute position

slides.title: Why the Rotation Trick Actually Works
slides.body: Rotation matrices compose by addition of angles: rotating by θ·m then θ·n is equivalent to a rotation by θ·(m-n) in the dot product. That algebraic property is the whole mechanism.
bullets: query at position m, key at position n → their dot product after rotation is a function of (m − n) alone
This gives relative position 'for free' out of an operation that only ever sees absolute positions m and n
No extra parameters are learned — the rotation angles are fixed by a formula, same family as the original sinusoidal scheme

rows: Poorly — unseen indices have no embedding
Somewhat, but attention still position-sensitive in absolute terms
Reasonably, and extendable further with scaling tricks

slides.title: Worked Example: A 4,000-Token Prompt on a 2k-Trained Model
slides.body: Take a model pretrained with a 2k-token context using RoPE. Feed it a 4,000-token prompt.
bullets: With learned absolute embeddings, tokens past position 2,048 simply have no trained embedding — quality degrades sharply or the model errors out

With RoPE, the model has still only 'seen' relative distances up to ~2,048 during training, so accuracy still degrades past that — but gracefully, because the mechanism generalizes the same rotation formula to unseen distances
This gap is exactly why techniques like Position Interpolation and YaRN exist: they rescale RoPE's rotation frequencies so a model trained at 2k can be adapted to 32k+ context with modest fine-tuning

slides.title: Trade-offs and Pitfalls
slides.body: RoPE isn't free of downsides, and swapping it in isn't always the right call.
bullets: Raw extrapolation still degrades beyond trained context length — RoPE helps, but doesn't solve long-context for free; scaling methods (YaRN, NTK-aware scaling) are usually still needed
The rotation is applied per-attention-head at every layer, which is a bit more compute than a one-time embedding addition (though the cost is small in practice)

For short, fixed-length sequences (e.g., a classifier with a hard 128-token cap), simpler absolute or learned embeddings can be perfectly fine — RoPE's benefit is specifically about relative structure and length generalization

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Self-attention has no inherent sense of order — position must be injected explicitly, and how you do it shapes how well the model generalizes
RoPE rotates query/key vectors by an angle proportional to position, so their dot product depends only on relative distance
That relative-distance property is why LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, and most modern open LLMs extrapolate to longer contexts better than absolute-position predecessors
RoPE alone doesn't solve long-context — pair it with scaling techniques like YaRN when you need to push well past the trained length

questions.prompt: Why does raw self-attention need an explicit positional encoding scheme at all?
options: Because attention layers are too slow without it
Because attention's dot-product weighting is order-blind — shuffling tokens gives the same output without positional info
Because GPUs can't process sequences in parallel otherwise
Because the softmax function requires sorted input
questions.explanation: Self-attention computes weighted sums over all tokens via dot products; with no positional signal, 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would look identical to the mechanism.

questions.prompt: What does RoPE actually do to encode position?
options: It adds a learned vector to each token embedding
It appends a position index as an extra token
It rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to position before the dot product
It sorts tokens by position before attention
questions.explanation: RoPE rotates Q and K in pairs of dimensions by θ·position. Because rotation angles compose by subtraction, the resulting dot product depends only on relative distance (m − n).

questions.prompt: Why do RoPE-based models generally extrapolate to longer contexts better than models with learned absolute position embeddings?
options: RoPE models use more parameters overall
Learned absolute embeddings have no representation at all for indices past the trained length; RoPE's rotation formula still generalizes to unseen relative distances
RoPE disables attention for tokens past the trained length
There's no real difference — both extrapolate equally well
questions.explanation: An absolute embedding for position 5,000 simply doesn't exist if training only went to 2,048 — there's nothing to look up. RoPE's rotation is a formula, not a lookup table, so it degrades more gracefully (though scaling tricks like YaRN are still typically needed for large jumps).

references.title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding (Su et al., 2021)

posts.title: Libra: Fixing the GPU Waste in Agent Training
posts.subtitle: A scheduler that stops one slow tool call from stalling 48 GPUs
posts.summary: Libra is a resource-management system for agentic RL post-training that jointly schedules GPUs across the rollout and training stages. By replacing fragile length-based scheduling with a causality-driven queue and letting GPUs move fluidly between stages, it reports up to 3.0x higher throughput and 2.5x faster reward convergence on a 48-GPU cluster.

slides.body: Reinforcement learning has become the standard way to turn a base LLM into a capable agent. A June 2026 paper argues the infrastructure underneath that process is badly underutilized — and shows a fix.
slides.title: Training an Agent Wastes Most of Its GPUs

slides.body: In agentic RL, the 'rollout' stage has the model call real tools — search, code execution, browsers — to generate training trajectories. Tool calls don't take a fixed amount of time.
bullets: Rollout workloads are long-tailed: a small fraction of trajectories dominates total rollout time
Sequence lengths drift continuously as the policy improves, so static length predictions go stale
Rollout and training stages have different compute, memory, and sequence-length needs, but share the same fixed GPU pool
slides.title: One Slow Trajectory Stalls the Whole Batch

slides.body: Most agentic RL setups statically partition GPUs between rollout and training. That split can't adapt when rollout suddenly needs more capacity because of a few slow trajectories, or when training temporarily idles waiting on rollout to finish.
bullets: Cross-stage imbalance in compute pattern and memory footprint
Fixed partitioning can't react to workload drift within a single training run
The bottleneck isn't the model — it's the scheduler underneath it
slides.title: Two Stages, One Rigid GPU Split

slides.body: Libra pairs a global resource planner with a new scheduling policy built around cause, not just predicted length.
bullets: Global resource planner jointly optimizes GPU allocation across rollout and training clusters
An elastic hybrid pool lets workers move between stages without blocking the run
A causality-driven multilevel feedback queue (C-MLFQ) routes trajectories to fast/slow buckets using signals from actual tool-return outcomes
That replaces brittle length-prediction heuristics, which break down as the policy — and its outputs — keep changing
slides.title: Libra's Two Mechanisms

rows: Time to reach a target reward

slides.body: Picture a batch of 256 rollout trajectories where a coding agent triggers a full test suite on 10 of them. Under static, length-based scheduling, the batch waits on those 10 slow test runs before training can start.
bullets: C-MLFQ detects the causal pattern — 'tool call returned late' — and demotes those trajectories to a slow-lane bucket
Fast trajectories keep flowing into training instead of queuing behind slow ones
The elastic pool can temporarily hand idle rollout GPUs to the training stage while the slow lane catches up
slides.title: Why a Coding Agent Benefits Most

bullets: Agentic RL's real bottleneck is often GPU scheduling, not model architecture
Static rollout/training GPU splits can't react to workload drift as the policy evolves
Libra routes work by causal signals (why a trajectory is slow) instead of fragile length predictions
Reported gains: up to 3.0x throughput and 2.5x faster convergence on 48 A800 GPUs
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: What specific problem does Libra target in agentic RL post-training?
options: Poor tokenizer efficiency
GPU scheduling imbalance between the rollout and training stages
A lack of labeled training data
Slow gradient descent convergence in supervised fine-tuning
questions.explanation: Libra is a resource-management system: it targets the mismatch in compute, memory, and timing needs between the rollout stage (tool-calling trajectory generation) and the training stage, which share a fixed GPU pool in most setups.

questions.prompt: Why does Libra avoid scheduling trajectories by predicted sequence length?
options: Sequence length is impossible to measure
Length predictions go stale because sequence-length distribution drifts as the policy evolves during training
Length-based scheduling is illegal under most cluster policies
GPUs cannot process variable-length sequences
questions.explanation: As the policy being trained changes, so does the distribution of trajectory lengths it produces — a length predictor calibrated early in training becomes unreliable later, which is why Libra's C-MLFQ scheduler instead uses causal signals from tool-return outcomes.

questions.prompt: On a 48 A800 GPU cluster, what throughput and convergence gains does the paper report for Libra versus baseline schedulers?
options: 1.2x throughput, no change in convergence speed
Up to 3.0x higher throughput and up to 2.5x faster reward convergence
10x throughput but slower convergence
No measurable throughput change, only lower memory usage
questions.explanation: The paper reports up to 3.0x higher throughput and up to 2.5x faster convergence in reward compared to baseline schedulers, evaluated on a 48 A800 GPU cluster.

references.title: Libra: Efficient Resource Management for Agentic RL Post-Training (arXiv:2606.03077)

posts.title: Claude Sonnet 5: The Agent Workhorse Gets an Upgrade
posts.subtitle: Not the smartest Claude — the one built to finish the whole job
posts.summary: Anthropic shipped Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30, 2026, closing much of the gap with Opus 4.8 on agentic and coding benchmarks while staying priced for high-volume agent workloads. The headline jump is reliability on long, multi-step tasks, not raw peak intelligence.

slides.body: On June 30, 2026, Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 — the newest entry in its mainstream Sonnet line, positioned as the model you run agents on all day, not just the one you reach for on the hardest problem.
slides.title: A Model Built to Finish, Not Just Answer

slides.body: Anthropic's own framing: a two-part enterprise task — update Salesforce account tiers, then send a launch announcement to enterprise contacts — used to stall halfway with Sonnet 4.6.
bullets: Long agent chains compound small errors across steps
Tool-use and terminal tasks were a particular weak point for Sonnet 4.6
Users needed Opus-tier reliability without Opus-tier cost for everyday agent work
slides.title: Multi-Step Agent Tasks Used to Stall

slides.body: The gains aren't evenly spread — they're concentrated exactly where agents fail most: tool use, terminal work, and computer control.
bullets: Terminal-bench: 76.1%, up from Sonnet 4.6's 55.4% — the single biggest jump, +20.7 points
OSWorld-Verified (computer use): 81.2%, up from 78.5%
GDPval-AA v2 (knowledge work): 1,618, edging out Opus 4.8's 1,615
Anthropic's safety testing found a lower rate of undesirable behaviors in agentic contexts than Sonnet 4.6
slides.title: What Actually Improved

slides.body: The same two-part CRM task — update account tiers across records, then draft and send an announcement to the right contact list — now completes without a human stepping in mid-way.
bullets: Sonnet 5 holds the full multi-step plan across the tool calls instead of losing the thread partway through
Both context window (1M tokens) and pricing are shared across Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 5, and Opus 4.8 on the API
The intro price cuts cost roughly 33% versus standard pricing through August 31, 2026
slides.title: The Salesforce Task, End to End

slides.body: Sonnet 5 isn't trying to beat Opus 4.8 on raw difficulty — it's trying to make the 90% of agent work that doesn't need Opus-level reasoning cheaper and more reliable.
bullets: Opus 4.8 still leads on the hardest reasoning: 69.2% vs. Sonnet 5's 63.2% on the hardest agentic-coding split
Sonnet 5 essentially matches or beats Opus 4.8 on knowledge-work and computer-use tasks at roughly 40% of the cost
The right choice depends on the task's ceiling, not just its category
slides.title: Why a Mid-Tier Model, Not a Bigger One

bullets: Claude Sonnet 5 (June 30, 2026) targets agent reliability, especially tool use and terminal work
Terminal-bench jumped +20.7 points over Sonnet 4.6 — the largest single gain
It undercuts Opus 4.8 on price by roughly 60% while matching it on some knowledge-work benchmarks
Opus 4.8 still wins on the hardest reasoning — pick Sonnet 5 for volume, Opus for ceiling
slides.title: Takeaways

options: SWE-bench Verified, +2 points

A new multimodal video benchmark

posts.title: Hybrid Search: Why Vector-Only RAG Misses Exact Matches
posts.subtitle: Combining BM25 and embeddings without ever comparing their scores
posts.summary: Vector search alone misses exact-match queries — product codes, error IDs, rare terms — because embeddings encode meaning, not literal strings. Hybrid search runs BM25 and dense retrieval in parallel and fuses their rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion, which sidesteps the fact that a cosine score and a BM25 score aren't comparable numbers.

slides.body: Most RAG pipelines retrieve with vector similarity alone. That works for meaning — and quietly fails for exact strings.
slides.title: Two Search Engines Are Better Than One

slides.body: Dense embeddings are trained to capture semantic similarity, not literal string overlap. That makes them weak on the queries where an exact match matters most.
bullets: A query for error code 'E4521' can rank a doc mentioning it 8th, behind semantically-similar but wrong docs
Rare product IDs, acronyms, and named entities often get smoothed away in embedding space
Most RAG failures traced in production are retrieval failures, not hallucinations — the model never saw the right chunk
slides.title: Vector-Only RAG Has a Blind Spot

slides.body: BM25 is a decades-old lexical scoring formula: it rewards exact term overlap, weighted by how rare and how discriminative each term is.
bullets: BM25 (sparse/lexical): excellent at exact terms, IDs, rare words; blind to paraphrase and synonyms
Dense vectors (semantic): excellent at paraphrase and concept matches; weak on exact rare-term recall
Neither one dominates the other — they fail on different queries
slides.title: BM25 and Dense Vectors Are Complementary

slides.body: The obvious approach — average a 0.85 cosine score with a 12.4 BM25 score — breaks immediately, because the two scores live on unrelated scales. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) sidesteps this by throwing scores away and using only rank position.
bullets: RRF score for a document = sum over each retriever of 1 / (k + rank), typically k = 60
A document ranked #1 by BM25 and #5 by vectors scores higher than one ranked #3 and #40
No score normalization, no manual weight tuning between retrievers required
slides.title: Fusing Ranks, Not Scores

slides.body: Query: 'error code E4521 timeout'. BM25 ranks the doc containing the literal string 'E4521' at #1 because it's a rare, highly discriminative term. The dense retriever ranks the same doc #8, since 'E4521' looks like generic noise in embedding space but a doc about 'connection timeout errors' looks more semantically relevant.
bullets: A purely semantic doc that BM25 never surfaces (rank absent) only gets credit from the vector list
The exact-match doc wins the fused ranking without either retriever needing to be reweighted by hand
slides.title: Worked Example: An Error-Code Query

rows: Two indexes to build and maintain

slides.body: Hybrid search adds real infrastructure cost — two indexes, two retrieval calls per query, and a fusion step to tune.
bullets: Small, homogeneous corpora (a few hundred FAQ docs) rarely need it — one method is usually enough
If your queries are almost never exact-match (pure conversational Q&A), dense-only may suffice
The k constant in RRF (commonly 60) still needs sanity-checking against your own query mix, not blind copy-paste
slides.title: When Hybrid Search Isn't Worth It

bullets: Vector-only RAG systematically underperforms on exact-match queries like IDs and rare terms
BM25 and dense retrieval fail in different, complementary ways — run both, not one
RRF fuses rankings using rank position only, avoiding the incompatible-score-scales problem
Skip hybrid search for small, low-exact-match corpora — the added infra isn't always worth it
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: Why does vector-only (dense embedding) retrieval often fail on queries containing exact IDs or rare technical terms?
options: Vector databases can't store short strings
Dense embeddings are trained to capture semantic similarity, not literal string overlap, so rare exact terms get smoothed away
Vector search is always slower than keyword search
Embeddings only work on English text
questions.explanation: Embeddings encode meaning, so a rare literal string like a product code or error ID doesn't stand out the way it does to a lexical method like BM25 — semantically 'similar' documents can outrank the one with the actual exact match.

questions.prompt: Why can't you just average a BM25 score and a cosine similarity score to combine rankings?
options: Averaging is computationally too expensive
The two scores live on different, incompatible scales, so a direct numeric combination is unreliable
BM25 scores are always negative
Cosine similarity cannot be computed for text
questions.explanation: A BM25 score (e.g. 12.4) and a cosine similarity (e.g. 0.85) aren't measuring the same thing on the same scale — naively averaging them lets whichever score happens to have a larger numeric range dominate the fusion.

questions.prompt: How does Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combine results from two retrievers?
options: It picks whichever retriever returned more results
It sums 1/(k + rank) for each document across each retriever's ranked list, using only rank position, not the underlying scores
It multiplies the raw scores from both retrievers together
It always trusts BM25 over vector search
questions.explanation: RRF discards raw scores entirely and computes a document's fused score from the reciprocal of its rank in each list (1/(k+rank), typically k=60), which sidesteps the score-incompatibility problem completely.

references.title: Advanced RAG — Understanding Reciprocal Rank Fusion in Hybrid Search
Hybrid Search Explained With An In-Depth Guide

posts.title: iLLaDA: Diffusion Language Models Catch Up
posts.subtitle: An 8B bidirectional diffusion model narrows the gap with autoregressive LLMs
posts.summary: iLLaDA is an 8B-parameter masked diffusion language model trained with fully bidirectional attention instead of the usual left-to-right causal attention. Scaled to 12T pretraining tokens, it posts double-digit point gains over its predecessor on reasoning, math, and code benchmarks.

slides.title: Two Ways to Write a Sentence
slides.body: Almost every LLM you use writes left to right, one token at a time. iLLaDA writes differently — and a new paper shows that approach finally catching up.

slides.title: Autoregressive Models Have a Ceiling
slides.body: Causal, left-to-right generation is simple and scales well, but a model can never revise a word it already committed to, and it can only attend to what came before it.
bullets: Generation is strictly sequential
Each token can only see earlier tokens
Errors early in a response can't be corrected

slides.title: What Masked Diffusion Does Instead
slides.body: Diffusion language models start from a fully masked sequence and iteratively 'unmask' tokens in parallel, attending in both directions at once — closer to how diffusion image models denoise pixels.
bullets: Bidirectional attention, not causal
Tokens can be revised as more context appears
Generation happens in parallel steps, not strictly left-to-right

slides.title: What iLLaDA Changes
slides.body: iLLaDA keeps the masked-diffusion objective through both pretraining and fine-tuning, scales to 12T pretraining tokens, fine-tunes on a 25B-token instruction corpus, and adds variable-length generation plus confidence-based scoring for evaluation.
bullets: 12T tokens of masked-diffusion pretraining
25B-token instruction fine-tuning, 12 epochs
Variable-length generation for efficiency
Confidence-based scoring for multiple-choice evals

slides.title: Why This Matters
slides.body: Because diffusion LLMs generate non-sequentially, they open the door to parallel decoding and easier self-correction — things autoregressive models can't do as naturally.
bullets: Parallel token generation is a structural, not just engineering, advantage
Bidirectional context can catch inconsistencies earlier
Still trails top autoregressive models overall, but the gap is shrinking fast

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Diffusion LLMs generate via iterative unmasking, not left-to-right
iLLaDA scales the masked-diffusion recipe to 12T pretraining tokens
Gains of 14-22 points across BBH, ARC, MATH, and HumanEval vs. its predecessor
Diffusion is emerging as a real architectural alternative to causal attention

questions.prompt: What's the core architectural difference between iLLaDA and a standard autoregressive LLM like GPT?
options: iLLaDA uses bidirectional attention and generates via iterative unmasking, not strict left-to-right decoding
iLLaDA has no attention mechanism at all
iLLaDA only works on images, not text
iLLaDA uses a smaller vocabulary
questions.explanation: Masked diffusion models attend in both directions and refine a fully-masked sequence over multiple steps, instead of committing to one token at a time left-to-right.

questions.prompt: iLLaDA scaled its masked-diffusion pretraining to roughly how many tokens?
options: 12 million
12 billion
12 trillion
12 quadrillion
questions.explanation: The paper scales pretraining to 12T tokens, keeping the masked-diffusion objective throughout instead of switching to a causal objective.

questions.prompt: According to the paper, iLLaDA-Base's biggest reported gain over LLaDA-Base was on:
options: BBH, +21.6 points
MATH, +14.5 points
HumanEval, +16.5 points
ARC-Challenge, +21.6 points
questions.explanation: iLLaDA-Base improved by 21.6 points on BBH and 14.9 points on ARC-Challenge over LLaDA-Base.

posts.title: Mixture of Experts: Big Model, Small Compute Bill
posts.subtitle: How frontier LLMs got bigger without getting proportionally slower
posts.summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures split a model's feed-forward layers into many specialized 'experts' and route each token to only a few of them. That lets a model have far more total parameters while only activating a small fraction per token — more capacity without a proportional compute bill.

slides.title: One Model, Many Specialists
slides.body: Frontier models like Mixtral and DeepSeek-V3 pack huge parameter counts — but only use a sliver of them per token. Here's how.

slides.title: Bigger Models Cost More Per Token
slides.body: In a dense model, every parameter does work on every single token. Doubling model size roughly doubles the compute cost of every forward pass — capacity and cost are locked together.
bullets: Dense models: every parameter fires on every token
More capacity always means more compute per token
Scaling up gets expensive fast

slides.title: Split the Layer Into Experts
slides.body: Mixture-of-Experts replaces a single large feed-forward block with many smaller 'expert' networks. A lightweight router picks a handful of experts for each token.
bullets: Dozens of expert sub-networks per layer
A router scores experts and picks the top-k (often 1-2)
Only the chosen experts run for that token

slides.title: Sparse Activation, Not Sparse Capacity
slides.body: The model's total parameter count includes every expert, but only a small fraction activate per token — so you get the memory footprint of a huge model with the compute cost of a much smaller one.
bullets: Total params: sum of all experts (huge)
Active params per token: just the chosen few (small)
The router is trained end-to-end with the rest of the model

rows: Simple, but cost scales with total size
More total capacity, similar per-token compute

slides.title: Where You've Seen It
slides.body: Mixtral 8x7B has 47B total parameters but activates only about 13B per token, routing each token to 2 of 8 experts per layer — while matching or beating much larger dense models on many benchmarks.
bullets: 8 experts per layer, top-2 routing
47B total params, ~13B active per token
Competitive with far larger dense models on several benchmarks

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: MoE decouples total capacity from per-token compute cost
A router picks a small top-k subset of experts per token
Memory footprint stays large even though compute per token shrinks
It's now a default lever for scaling frontier LLMs efficiently

questions.prompt: What problem does Mixture-of-Experts primarily solve?
options: It removes the need for attention layers
It decouples a model's total parameter capacity from its per-token compute cost
It eliminates the need for GPUs entirely
It compresses the vocabulary size
questions.explanation: MoE lets a model hold far more total parameters while only activating a small subset of them for each token, so capacity grows without a proportional rise in compute per token.

questions.prompt: In an MoE layer, what does the 'router' do?
options: It compresses the model's weights for storage
It scores the available experts and selects a small top-k subset to process each token
It schedules GPU jobs across a data center
It converts tokens into embeddings
questions.explanation: The router is a small learned network that decides which experts (usually just 1-2 out of many) should process each individual token.

questions.prompt: Why does an MoE model still need a lot of memory even though it activates few parameters per token?
options: Because all experts must be loaded even though only a few are used per token
Because MoE models store raw training data in memory
Because routers require more memory than the experts themselves
Because MoE models don't support quantization
questions.explanation: Every expert is a real set of weights that must be resident in memory to be available for routing, even though any single token only exercises a handful of them.

posts.title: KV Cache Quantization: 6x More Context for Free
posts.subtitle: How compressing an LLM's memory unlocks longer context without new hardware
posts.summary: KV cache quantization shrinks the memory an LLM uses to remember prior tokens, letting servers hold far more context per GPU without hurting accuracy. It's becoming a default lever in production inference stacks.

slides.title: The Hidden Memory Tax
slides.body: Every token an LLM generates re-reads every previous token's key and value vectors. That KV cache is often the single biggest consumer of GPU memory — bigger than the model weights.

slides.title: Context Is Expensive
slides.body: A 128K-token conversation can require tens of gigabytes of KV cache per request. More context means fewer concurrent users per GPU, or costly hardware upgrades.
bullets: KV cache grows linearly with context length
Stored in full 16-bit precision by default
Directly limits batch size and concurrency

slides.title: What Quantization Does
slides.body: Instead of storing each key/value number in 16 bits, quantization rounds it to a smaller number of bits (e.g. 3-4) while preserving the range it represents.
bullets: Trades numeric precision for memory savings
Applied per-channel or per-block to limit error
Decoded back to full precision just before attention math

slides.title: Why It (Mostly) Works
slides.body: KV cache values tend to cluster in a narrow range, so aggressive quantization loses very little signal — recent techniques report near-zero measured accuracy loss even at 3 bits.
bullets: Smart calibration keeps outlier values accurate
Attention scores are surprisingly tolerant of rounding
Combines with paged/chunked cache management

slides.title: Real-World Payoff
slides.body: Google's TurboQuant approach (2026) compresses KV cache to 3 bits with no measured accuracy loss, delivering roughly 6x memory reduction — so one GPU can serve far more long-context requests.
bullets: 6x more concurrent long-context sessions
Lower cost per token served
Stacks with speculative decoding and paged attention

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: KV cache, not weights, is often the real memory bottleneck
Quantizing it trades a sliver of precision for major capacity gains
3-4 bit KV cache quantization is becoming a production default
Pair it with batching and speculative decoding for compounding wins

questions.prompt: What part of an LLM's inference workload does KV cache quantization primarily target?
options: The model's trained weights
The stored key/value vectors from prior tokens
The tokenizer vocabulary
The final output logits
questions.explanation: KV cache holds the key/value vectors for every previous token so attention can reference them; quantization compresses that specific store, not the model weights.

questions.prompt: Why does aggressive KV cache quantization (e.g. 3-4 bits) often lose very little accuracy?
options: LLMs ignore most of the context anyway
KV values cluster in a narrow range, so rounding loses little signal
Quantization only applies to unused tokens
GPUs automatically correct rounding errors
questions.explanation: Key/value activations tend to have a narrow, predictable range, so lower-precision representations retain almost all the useful signal, especially with good calibration.

questions.prompt: What's the main practical benefit of shrinking KV cache memory?
options: Faster model training
Smaller download size for the app
More concurrent long-context requests per GPU
Elimination of hallucinations
questions.explanation: Less memory per request means a server can hold more simultaneous or longer conversations on the same hardware, directly cutting cost per token served.

posts.title: MCP Goes Stateless: The July RC Rewrite
posts.subtitle: No more sticky sessions — MCP servers finally play nice with load balancers
posts.summary: The July 2026 MCP release candidate strips session state out of the protocol, so any server instance can answer any request. That means remote MCP servers can finally sit behind a plain load balancer before the spec locks on July 28.

slides.title: MCP Goes Stateless
slides.body: A protocol-level rewrite lands this week — and it changes how you'll deploy every remote MCP server you run.

slides.title: The old MCP had a sticky problem
bullets: Sessions lived inside one server process
Load balancers had to pin you to that exact instance
Scale out = session affinity hacks everywhere
One instance dies, your whole session dies with it

slides.title: What 'stateless' actually removes
slides.body: The RC deletes session state from the protocol layer. The server no longer remembers you between calls — the client carries the context instead.

slides.title: Before vs. after, structurally
bullets: Before: client → LB → pinned server → in-memory session
After: client → LB → any healthy server → context in the request
Server pool becomes fully interchangeable
Crashes just mean a retry, not a lost session

rows: Works behind a plain round-robin LB

slides.title: Deploying a remote server this week
slides.body: Drop server replicas behind nginx or an ALB with no session affinity rule. Any node can pick up the next request — no shared session store required.

slides.title: Before July 28
bullets: Audit servers for in-memory session assumptions
Remove sticky-session config from your LB
Push per-call context into the request payload
Re-test failover — a dead node should now be a non-event

questions.prompt: What does the July 2026 MCP RC remove at the protocol level?

questions.prompt: Why did stateful MCP servers struggle behind load balancers?

questions.prompt: In the stateless model, where does per-call context now live?

Carried in the request itself

posts.title: Context Engineering: Why Agents Fail
posts.subtitle: The model didn't get dumber. Its window got messier.
posts.summary: As agents run longer, the context window fills with stale tool outputs, contradictions, and irrelevant history — and that clutter, not the model's intelligence, is what breaks reasoning. Great agent builders now spend most of their effort curating what enters the window at each step, not wordsmithing the initial prompt.

slides.title: Context Engineering
slides.body: Why agents fail long before the model does.

slides.title: The Model Isn't The Bottleneck Anymore
bullets: Same model, wildly different agent results
The real variable: what's IN the context window
Bad context in → bad output out, every time

slides.title: What Is Context Engineering?
slides.body: Not how you phrase one prompt — the ongoing discipline of deciding what enters the window at every single step of an agent's run.

slides.title: Four Ways Context Breaks
bullets: Poisoning — one bad fact gets referenced forever
Distraction — too much history buries the signal
Confusion — irrelevant tools/docs mislead the model
Clash — conflicting facts fight each other

rows: Optimizes the wording of one message
Optimizes what's loaded at each step
Focus: memory, tools, retrieved docs, history

slides.title: A Real Agent Loop, Turn By Turn
bullets: Step 1: task + 3 relevant files — clean
Step 12: + 40 tool outputs, stack traces, dead plans
Step 20: model 'forgets' the goal — it's buried

slides.title: Engineer The Window, Not Just The Words
bullets: Prune aggressively after every tool call
Compress and summarize instead of appending forever
Retrieve just-in-time, not just-in-case
Isolate sub-tasks into fresh sub-agent contexts

questions.prompt: In mature 2026 agentic frameworks, what has become the primary bottleneck for agent reliability?
options: The model's raw reasoning ability
What gets loaded into the context window at each step
The programming language used to build the agent
GPU inference speed
questions.explanation: Model capability has plateaued relative to agent failures — the deciding factor is now what content the agent stuffs into its own context at every turn.

questions.prompt: What is 'context poisoning'?
options: When a hallucinated or wrong fact from an earlier step keeps getting referenced in later steps
When the model is prompted in an unsupported language
When the context window exceeds the token limit
When two agents share the same API key
questions.explanation: Once an error enters the window, the model treats it as established fact and builds on it, compounding the mistake across subsequent steps.

questions.prompt: What's a practical fix for context distraction?
options: Add more tool descriptions to the system prompt
Compress and prune history, and retrieve just-in-time instead of dumping everything upfront
Increase the model's temperature
Switch to a smaller, cheaper model
questions.explanation: Distraction happens when accumulated history drowns out the actual task — active pruning and lazy retrieval keep the signal-to-noise ratio high.
